questions.prompt: Read: "The candidate who got the job is the one whose CV impressed the panel and who speaks three languages." Which statement is true?

options.text: The candidate speaks only one language.
Nobody was given the job.
The panel disliked the candidate's CV.
The person who was hired speaks three languages.

questions.explanation: The clauses describe one candidate: who got the job, whose CV impressed the panel, and who speaks three languages — so (d) is true. (a) contradicts "three languages", (b) contradicts "got the job", and (c) contradicts "impressed the panel".

questions.prompt: In which sentence can the relative pronoun NOT be left out?

options.text: The song (that) she sang was beautiful.
The people (who) we invited didn't come.
The train that leaves at noon is always full.
The letter (which) I wrote got lost.

questions.explanation: In (c) the pronoun is the subject (a verb, "leaves", follows), so it must stay: the train that leaves at noon. In (a), (b) and (d) a subject (she, we, I) follows, making the pronoun the object — which can be dropped.

questions.prompt: Combine into one sentence: "They bought a house. The roof of the house was damaged."

options.text: They bought a house which roof was damaged.
They bought a house whose roof was damaged.
They bought a house who roof was damaged.
They bought a house where roof was damaged.

questions.explanation: whose shows possession and works for things too, replacing "of the house": a house whose roof was damaged. "which" can't sit before a noun like this (a), "who" is for people (c), and "where" marks a place, not possession (d).

options.text: The colleague whose idea we used got a bonus.
The colleague who's idea we used got a bonus.
The colleague which idea we used got a bonus.
The colleague what idea we used got a bonus.

questions.explanation: whose shows possession (their idea): the colleague whose idea we used. "who's" means "who is" (b), "which" is for things and can't be possessive here (c), and "what" is never a relative pronoun (d).

questions.prompt: Complete: "I work with people ___ come from a country ___ four languages are spoken."

options.text: which … whose
whose … who
that … which
who … where

questions.explanation: "people" are persons and the subject of "come" (who), and a country is a place where languages are spoken (where). So: who … where. The other pairs mismatch — people are never "which/whose" as the doer, and "in a country which" would need the place as an object.

options.text: The town where I was born has a famous market.
The author whose novel won the prize is only twenty.
The thing what surprised me most was the silence.
The man who fixed my car charged too much.

questions.explanation: In (c), "the thing what surprised me" is wrong: never use "what" as a relative pronoun — say the thing that surprised me. (a) uses where for a place, (b) uses whose for possession, and (d) uses who for a person — all correct.
